**Research justification (from Cohen/Knowles amyloid master-equation theory, which I know from the field):** For Abeta42 the reduced aggregate-mass ODE is dominated by *secondary nucleation* — new aggregates form autocatalytically on the surface of existing fibrils — giving an autocatalytic term whose rate scales with existing mass `c` and free monomer `m`. Surface-acting inhibitors (e.g. Brichos, affibodies) act by binding the fibril surface and blocking the secondary-nucleation catalytic sites; the number of such sites is finite, so the *effective rate suppression saturates* with dose (Langmuir occupancy) and the half-time delay is bounded rather than divergent. The plateau (endpoint mass) is left essentially unchanged. This

The Langmuir saturation reflects the finite fibril-surface binding-site pool so high-dose held-out curves are not over-delayed — a robustness advantage for the held-out-curve generalization diagnostic.
